steps.title: Identify the passage's central argument
steps.content:
Emerson calls scholars the "priests of that thought which establishes the foundations of the earth" and says they "stand for the spiritual interest of the world." The danger he flags is specific to America: because "material interest is so predominant," scholars neglecting their duty is a "common calamity." The main idea pairs two elements—America's materialistic focus and scholars' obligation to preserve ideas.
  • Choice A — Military experience encourages Americans to contemplate aspects of human life that they would not choose to otherwise.: The passage never mentions military experience. ✗
  • Choice B — It is unfortunate that so many intellectuals are concerned with material things rather than ideas.: Emerson does not say scholars are themselves preoccupied with material things; he warns them not to neglect their duty amid a materialistic culture. ✗
  • Choice C — Many descriptions of the role of scholars in society unfairly diminish their importance.: The passage makes no reference to other descriptions of scholars. ✗
  • Choice D — In a country whose citizens are largely preoccupied with tangible gains, it is crucial that some people work to foster and preserve ideas.: This matches both elements: America's material preoccupation and scholars' essential role in preserving ideas. ✓

steps.title: Confirm the match
steps.content: The correct answer captures Emerson's argument precisely—the material dominance of American culture makes scholars' intellectual stewardship not optional but crucial. That confirms D is correct.
